one_line_takeaway: The paper proposes and empirically validates (63 LoRA-distilled students, 35,343 judge-scored samples) a new antidistillation watermark locus -- a system-prompt-induced behavioral marker in the teacher's interaction style rather than in token logits or model weights -- showing it transfers across model families, survives non-adaptive prompt paraphrasing to a meaningful degree, and remains undetected by users at low density, while explicitly leaving adaptive/response-side removal attacks as unaddressed future work.
key_finding: Phase 1 (within-family, regex proxy): watermarked-trained student produces question-ending responses 99.56% of the time (450/452) vs 5.09% (23/452) for a baseline-trained student, a +94.47pp gap (Wilson 95% CI [92.35, 96.59]). Phase 2 (cross-family, 63 students): against the teacher's 90.93% STRONG marker rate, relative transfer is 88.9% (Gemma), 80.9% (OLMo), 45.2% (Qwen) -- supporting H1 (learnability) and H2 (cross-family generalization); judge-human Cohen's kappa = 0.84 (STRONG rubric, 95% CI [0.72,0.94]) and 0.78 (STYLE rubric). Paraphrase robustness (H3): DIPPER degrades the teacher's own STRONG rate from 90.93% to 60.37% (66.4% retention) and SOFT from 17.85% to 11.93% (66.8% retention), setting a ceiling; student-relative retention R_rel on STRONG is 89+/-23% (Gemma), 112+/-16% (OLMo, exceeding the teacher itself), 65+/-30% (Qwen); on SOFT retention drops to 21-81%. Density-detectability dose-response (H4): at rho=20% (SOFT), detection is reliably above baseline for all families (e.g. OLMo 31.99+/-3.04% vs baseline 0.65+/-0.10%, a 49x lift). Stealth (H5, N=20 in-lab study): all four marker conditions sit within 0.22 Likert points of the 3.55 baseline mean, and TOST/Friedman/Bonferroni-Wilcoxon all support equivalence -- i.e. a low-density marker is not perceived as degrading interaction quality. A secondary finding (F-Style) is that a purely declarative marker (STYLE-CONTROL, no question mark) also transfers (Gemma 11.71%, Qwen 7.38%, OLMo 6.66%, all several-fold above baseline), which the authors argue is inconsistent with a purely surface-token-imitation account. A single-family anomaly (F-Amp) shows OLMo's SOFT-trained student detecting the marker at 179.2% relative rate (above the teacher itself), presented as a hypothesis-level, non-mechanistic observation rather than an established mechanism.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'interaction-layer' antidistillation watermarks for LLM APIs: instead of biasing next-token logits (green-list/cryptographic watermarks) or editing model weights, the defender wraps a teacher model with a system prompt that intermittently induces a behavioral marker B in its responses (e.g. an explicit trailing follow-up question 'STRONG', a low-density ~20% variant 'SOFT', or an embedded declarative restatement 'STYLE-CONTROL' with no question mark). Because the marker lives in the response text the teacher emits, an attacker who harvests prompt-response pairs and fine-tunes a student via knowledge distillation inherits the behavioral tendency even without access to teacher logits. The defender later audits a suspect student via black-box queries and a structured-output LLM-as-judge. The paper formalizes the watermark as a triple (B, Pi_B, rho), defines absolute/relative transfer rates tau/tau_rel and a two-level paraphrase-robustness decomposition R_S = R_T * R_rel (teacher-self ceiling times student-relative retention), and evaluates the approach empirically across two phases: (1) a within-family Qwen3.5-9B-Instruct -> Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base case study using a deterministic question-mark regex, and (2) a cross-family study of 3 base student families (Gemma-3-1B-pt, OLMo-2-0425-1B, Qwen3.5-0.8B-Base) x 7 system-prompt conditions x 3 seeds = 63 LoRA-distilled students trained on a shared Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct teacher, judged with an open-weights gpt-oss-120b LLM-as-judge (35,343 judged samples total), plus an N=20 pre-registered in-lab Latin-square user study on perceived interaction quality.
threat_model: Defender: an LLM API provider who controls only the system-prompt policy wrapping the teacher model at serving time; the defender does not control the attacker's training pipeline and, in the API setting, has no access to next-token logits. Attacker: harvests prompt-response text pairs from the teacher API (black-box, response-text only) and fine-tunes/distills a smaller student model without authorization, then ships it. The attacker in this paper's main evaluation is 'oblivious' -- unaware of the hidden trigger policy and not actively trying to strip the watermark, except for the paraphrase-robustness experiments where the attacker applies DIPPER to paraphrase user prompts before re-querying the teacher (a non-adaptive removal attempt). The paper explicitly notes that response-side rewriting attacks and adaptive attacks that specifically target the marker are NOT evaluated and are flagged as the most pressing open extension (Appendix O.1). Detection/audit is via black-box queries to the suspect student plus a structured-output LLM-as-judge (gpt-oss-120b) validated against 3 human annotators, blind to model family/condition/teacher.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) only non-adaptive prompt-side paraphrasing (DIPPER) was tested as a removal attack; response-side rewriting and adaptive attacks that specifically target the known marker are not evaluated and are called out as the most pressing extension (Appendix O.1). (2) The mechanism is dual-use/disclosed: if the marker design is known, an attacker can prompt-engineer or post-filter to remove it (Broader Impact, Appendix A); the authors explicitly frame the work as bounded empirical findings, not a production-ready defense. (3) The within-family Phase-1 case study uses a single seed and a deliberately strict regex proxy (literal trailing '?'), so it is a lower bound and does not itself address cross-family generalization, paraphrase robustness, or low density. (4) F-Amp (OLMo's >100% relative transfer / amplification) is explicitly labeled a hypothesis, not a validated mechanism -- only one data point (rho=100% vs rho=20%) is available and the authors call for a multi-point density scan to falsify their candidate explanation. (5) Judge-human agreement is imperfect and asymmetric: judge slightly under-detects on STRONG (cell kappa=0.62 in one 16-item subset), and STYLE-CONTROL prevalence-reweighted kappa drops to 0.40 at 3.7% yes-prevalence with a lowest per-family value of 0.64 (Qwen); these are reported by the authors as caveats on the reliability bound. (6) The H5 stealth study is an informal in-lab study (N=20, lab members, no external recruitment), not a large-scale crowdsourced replication -- the authors note this is 'pending crowdsourced replication.' (7) Family-ordering of transfer rates is inconsistent across conditions (STRONG: Gemma>OLMo>Qwen; SOFT: OLMo>>Gemma~Qwen; STYLE: Gemma>Qwen>OLMo), and no single mechanistic explanation is confirmed for these differences -- three candidate factors are offered without a discriminating experiment.

datasets_used: an open-chat slice (unspecified source, teacher prompt mixture)
quantitative_results: Phase 1 within-family: watermarked student question-mark rate 99.56% (450/452) vs baseline 5.09% (23/452), gap +94.47pp (95% CI [92.35,96.59])
Teacher achieved marker rates: STRONG 90.93%, SOFT 91.45%, STYLE-CONTROL 83.15% (on respective condition subsets) vs baseline 0.20%/1.20%
H1/H2 relative transfer on STRONG: Gemma 88.9%, OLMo 80.9%, Qwen 45.2% (vs teacher 90.93%)
Judge-human Cohen's kappa: 0.84 (STRONG rubric, 95% CI [0.72,0.94]), 0.78 (STYLE rubric); per-family STRONG kappa: Gemma 0.87, OLMo 0.74, Qwen 0.94
H3 DIPPER paraphrase teacher-self retention: STRONG 90.93%->60.37% (66.4% retention), SOFT 17.85%->11.93% (66.8% retention)
H3 student-relative retention R_rel on STRONG: Gemma 89+/-23%, OLMo 112+/-16%, Qwen 65+/-30%; on SOFT: 21-81% retention range
H4 dose-response at rho=20% (SOFT) vs baseline: Gemma 15.52% vs 0.00% (Wilson upper bound 0.23%, lift >=15.3pp); OLMo 31.99+/-3.04% vs 0.65+/-0.10% (49x); Qwen 15.98+/-0.88% vs 1.67+/-0.45% (9.6x)
H5 in-lab study (N=20): baseline mean Likert 3.55; deltas -- strong-20 -0.10, strong-50 +0.00, style-20 +0.15, style-50 +0.20 (all within 0.22 Likert steps)
F-Style detection rates: Gemma 11.71% (7.9x baseline), OLMo 6.66% (4.5x), Qwen 7.38% (4.1x)
F-Amp: OLMo SOFT relative transfer 179.2% (31.99% student vs 17.85% teacher); OLMo STRONG-under-paraphrase retention 112% of teacher's own retention
Total scale: 3 families x 7 conditions x 3 seeds = 63 LoRA-distilled students, 35,343 judge-evaluated samples
baselines_compared: Baseline (no-marker) system prompt condition, per family and per rubric
Teacher's own marker rate as the transfer-rate reference (tau_rel = tau(S,B)/tau(T,B))
Teacher's own paraphrased marker rate as the paraphrase-robustness reference (teacher-self ceiling R_T)

Qualitative comparison to prior antidistillation defense layers: token-distribution watermarks (green-list/KGW, cryptographic, undetectable, antidistillation sampling), model-layer fingerprinting (instructional fingerprinting, defensive output generation), and reasoning-trace-layer defenses (trace rewriting, PART) -- discussed as related work, not re-implemented/re-run as experimental baselines in this paper
